real_meaning: ～ところ expresses the specific 'point in time' or 'stage of an action' — before, during, or after — with surgical precision. The form of the verb attached tells you exactly where in the timeline you are: about to (dictionary form), in the middle of (～ている), or just finished (～た). It's less about describing an action and more about spotlighting the exact temporal moment you're catching someone or something at.

formation.rule: Attach ところ to: (1) dictionary form verb → about to do [<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるところ = just about to eat], (2) ～ている form → in the middle of doing [<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べているところ = in the middle of eating], (3) ～た form → just finished doing [<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたところ = just ate]. Can be followed by だ、です、or particles like に/を.
formation.common_mistake: Using the ～た form when you mean 'about to' — e.g., writing ×<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたところです to mean 'I'm about to eat' instead of ○<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるところです. The ～た form means you JUST finished, not that you're about to start.

sentences.jp: ちょうど<ruby>出<rt>で</rt></ruby>かけるところだったのに、<ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>ってきた。
sentences.translation: I was just about to head out when it started raining.
sentences.notes: The dictionary form + ところ captures that frustrating 'right at the moment of' feeling — the だった makes it past tense, showing the timing was interrupted.

sentences.jp: すみません、<ruby>今<rt>いま</rt></ruby>ちょうど<ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>準備<rt>じゅんび</rt></ruby>をしているところです。
sentences.translation: Sorry, I'm right in the middle of preparing for a meeting.
sentences.notes: ～ているところです politely signals 'I'm currently occupied with this specific task' — more precise and less abrupt than just ～ています.

sentences.jp: <ruby>只今<rt>ただいま</rt></ruby>、<ruby>担当者<rt>たんとうしゃ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>確認<rt>かくにん</rt></ruby>しているところでございます。
sentences.translation: Our representative is currently in the process of checking on that.
sentences.notes: In formal/business contexts ～ているところでございます is a standard way to signal a task is actively underway without giving a completion time.

sentences.jp: <ruby>今日<rt>きょう</rt></ruby>、ちょうど<ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>のことを<ruby>話<rt>はな</rt></ruby>していたところに、<ruby>本人<rt>ほんにん</rt></ruby>から<ruby>連絡<rt>れんらく</rt></ruby>が<ruby>来<rt>き</rt></ruby>た。
sentences.translation: We were literally just talking about him when he contacted us out of nowhere.
sentences.notes: ところに (not just ところだ) means something arrived or happened AT that exact moment — a slightly different construction that shows ところ can also function as a temporal anchor for surprising coincidences.

confused_with.contrast: Both ～たところ and ～たばかり describe something that recently happened, but they differ in focus and flexibility. ～たところ emphasizes the pinpoint freshness of the moment — 'literally just now, seconds ago' — while ～たばかり is more elastic and can mean 'recently' on a scale of minutes, hours, or even days depending on context. Minimal pair: <ruby>起<rt>お</rt></ruby>きたところです (I literally just woke up — still in bed, hair messy) vs. <ruby>起<rt>お</rt></ruby>きたばかりです (I just woke up — could be 20 minutes ago, explains why I look tired). Use ～たところ when the immediacy is the whole point; use ～たばかり when 'recently' is what matters.

bunpro_tip: Think of ところ as a camera shutter: the verb form is your shutter speed — dictionary form catches the moment just BEFORE the action, ている catches it IN motion, and た catches it the instant it ENDS. When you see a ところ sentence on BunPro, immediately look left at the verb form to set your mental clock.
